questions.prompt: Why use structured JSON logging over plain console.log strings?

choices.text: JSON logs are queryable — external sinks (Logflare, Axiom, Datadog) parse fields like level, userId, traceId, duration as columns you can filter and aggregate on. Plain strings force regex parsing or full-text search, both lossy and slow. With JSON, `wrangler tail --search '"level":"error"'` works trivially, and dashboards can chart p95 latency per route. The cost is one wrapper function; the payoff is logs you can actually answer questions with at scale.

questions.explanation: JSON logs become queryable fields in external sinks; plain strings are unstructured and lossy.

questions.prompt: Multi-service Worker request fails intermittently. Logs exist at each layer but you can't tell which logs belong to the failing request. Fix?

choices.text: Propagate a request ID (traceId) across service boundaries. Generate it at the edge entrypoint (`crypto.randomUUID()` or use an incoming X-Trace-Id header), pass it through Service Binding fetches via headers, log it as a field at every layer. Now `traceId:abc123` in your log sink shows the full path of one request across all Workers. Without correlation IDs, multi-service debugging is guess-and-check; with them, you follow the exact request through the system. Required pattern past a single Worker.

questions.explanation: Propagate a request ID via headers; log it at each layer to correlate logs across services.
